record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Town Clerk (marriages recorded at town level in New England tradition)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1892 (state registration); town clerks recorded from settlement

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Maine; Maine State Archives county records guides; Maine Genealogical Society resources
administrative_notes: Maine was the 23rd state, admitted March 15, 1820 under the Missouri Compromise. Previously the District of Maine within Massachusetts (from 1652/1658). All pre-1820 records are Massachusetts records — check Massachusetts State Archives and county courts. York County (1636) is the oldest. Lincoln County is the critical parent county for much of central and eastern Maine. Town clerks are primary vital record keepers following the New England pattern. French Canadian immigration (Acadian and Québécois) is extremely significant, especially in Aroostook County and mill cities like Lewiston and Biddeford. Maritime and fishing records are important for coastal counties.

core_repositories.description: Central state repository. Holds vital records (births, marriages, deaths from 1892), military records, legislative records, court records, naturalization records, and town reports. Microfilmed town records and county probate records.

Major genealogical and historical research library. Holds manuscript collections, family papers, town histories, photographs, maps, and the MHS Research Library. Extensive holdings on Portland and Cumberland County families.
Genealogy collection including Maine town histories, vital records, census indexes, city directories, and newspaper microfilm. Free access to Ancestry Library Edition on-site.
Publishes The Maine Genealogist quarterly. Maintains surname files, Bible records, and cemetery transcriptions. Partners with Maine State Library.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including vital records, probate records, land records, cemetery records, and town records. Significant microfilm holdings from Maine State Archives partnership.
Wiki guide to county-level records availability with links to digitized microfilm for individual counties.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Maine newspapers from the 1820s forward. Includes major papers like the Portland Eastern Argus, Bangor Daily Whig & Courier, and small-town weeklies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Maine State Library / University of Maine
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Extensive newspaper microfilm collection. University of Maine Fogler Library holds the largest collection of Maine newspapers including the Bangor Daily News and many county weeklies.

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from Maine courts (1790-1991). Declarations of intention and petitions for citizenship filed in county courts. Critical for tracing French Canadian, Irish, and other immigrant origins. Partially indexed.

French Canadian and Franco-American genealogical resources. Parish registers from Catholic churches in Lewiston, Biddeford, and other mill cities. Critical for Québécois and Acadian family research. PRDH (Programme de recherche en démographie historique) connections.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: NARA — Portland, Maine Port of Entry Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Passenger arrival records for Port of Portland and other Maine ports. Immigration across the Canadian border at Maine points of entry (Calais, Houlton, Jackman, Van Buren, Fort Fairfield). Canadian border crossing records are particularly rich.

military_records.description: Civil War service records and regimental histories (Maine contributed heavily — 20th Maine at Gettysburg). Also War of 1812, Mexican War, Spanish-American War, WWI, and WWII records. Maine Adjutant General reports published 1861-1866.
Collections related to Maine military history including artifacts, documents, and photographs from all conflicts. Camp Keyes records.

land_and_property.source_name: Bureau of Land Management — GLO Records (Maine)
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Maine. Note: Much of Maine's land was distributed through Massachusetts land grants (pre-1820) and proprietary claims rather than the federal homestead system. The Bingham Purchase, Knox Patent, and other large grants shaped land ownership patterns.
State land office records from 1820 forward. Includes lottery lands, settling lands, and grants in northern and eastern Maine. Critical for Aroostook County settlement research.

vital_records.repository: Maine CDC, Office of Data, Research, and Vital Statistics
vital_records.description: State-level vital records: births, marriages, deaths from 1892 forward. Pre-1892 records are at the town level — contact individual town clerks. Historical records available through Maine State Archives.

vital_records.repository: Individual Town Clerks / Maine State Archives
vital_records.description: Town clerks recorded births, marriages, and deaths from earliest settlement following the New England tradition. Many have been transcribed and published. The Maine Old Cemetery Association and DAR chapters transcribed extensively. FamilySearch has microfilmed many town records.

church_and_cemetery.description: Systematic transcription of Maine cemetery inscriptions. Published county-by-county volumes. Holdings at Maine State Library. Supplements Find A Grave coverage.
Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Maine. Coastal and rural town cemeteries extensively photographed.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Catholic Parish Records — Diocese of Portland
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic parish records for all of Maine (single diocese). Sacramental registers (baptism, marriage, burial) especially important for Franco-American, Irish, and Italian families. Many French-language parishes in mill cities.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Comprehensive research guide covering Maine record types, repositories, county formation history, and research strategies.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, obituary lookups, and county-specific research guides.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: University of Maine — Fogler Library Special Collections
research_guides_and_societies.description: Maine manuscript collections, town histories, lumbering records, maritime records, and the Northeast Archives of Folklore and Oral History. Strong holdings for Penobscot and Piscataquis counties.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Maine has no extinct counties. All 16 current counties have been in continuous existence since their formation. The county formation sequence is: York (1636/1652), Cumberland and Lincoln (1760), Hancock and Washington (1789/1790), Kennebec and Oxford and Somerset (1799), Penobscot (1816), Waldo (1827), Franklin and Piscataquis (1838), Aroostook (1839), Knox and Sagadahoc and Androscoggin (1860). Cornwall County (1665) briefly existed under the Duke of York's grant but was superseded. Pre-1820 all Maine counties were Massachusetts jurisdictions — check Massachusetts records for earlier periods.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Maine has no counties classified as 'burned counties' in the genealogical sense. Record survival is generally excellent across all 16 counties. Some minor localized losses occurred (e.g., individual town records lost in fires) but no county experienced catastrophic courthouse destruction. The New England tradition of town-level record keeping means records are distributed across many repositories rather than concentrated in a single courthouse.
